title: The AI-Native Glossary
subtitle: Lifestyles of the AI-Natives — every term behind voice workflows, agentic loops, and scheduled tasks.

sections.name: The ground floor
sections.intro: The handful of words everything else stands on. Five minutes here and the rest of the page reads easy.

terms.definition: A model plus a harness. The model does the reasoning and makes the decisions; the harness is everything that lets it act — tools, permissions, the loop that keeps it moving. When those two run in a loop toward a goal, you have an agent.

Claude running in your terminal, with access to your files. It reads, edits, runs commands, and drives Git — doing, not just talking. It's the most capable place to work with Claude because it lives where the work lives.
The AI itself: a very large mathematical system, trained on enormous amounts of text, that predicts useful output one piece at a time. Claude is a family of models.

Claude's model tiers. Opus is the deepest thinker — strongest on hard problems, slower and pricier. Sonnet is the workhorse balance most work runs on. Haiku is fast and cheap, for high-volume simple tasks. A sane default: start on the standard tier, reach for Opus when the problem deserves it.
terms.hear: “Sonnet couldn't crack it — kick it to Opus and check back after lunch.”

terms.definition: The unit a model reads and writes text in — roughly three-quarters of an English word. Pricing, speed, and memory limits are all measured in tokens, which is why people who run AI talk about them constantly.
terms.hear: “That run burned a lot of tokens.”

terms.definition: The model's working memory: everything it can see at one moment — your conversation so far, files it has read, its instructions. It's big but finite, and when it fills up, older material falls out. Most “it forgot what I said an hour ago” mysteries are context-window mysteries.

When a model states something false with full confidence — citing a clause that isn't in the file, inventing a number. Far rarer in current frontier models, and largely preventable: give the model the real source material and ask it to verify its work against something checkable.

The standard connector that plugs Claude into other software — Slack, Notion, your database, your CRM. One plug standard, adopted across the industry, so any AI can use any MCP-wired system. When someone asks “is there an MCP for that?” they're asking “can Claude reach that system?”

sections.name: Voice workflows
sections.intro: The keyboard was always the narrow pipe between what you wanted and what got built. Voice widens it.

terms.definition: Driving Claude Code by speaking instead of typing; you say the outcome, the agent writes the code. The keyboard stops being the bottleneck between intent and change. You describe what you want done and review what comes back, the way you'd review any teammate's work.
terms.hear: “I haven't touched the keyboard in an hour.”

terms.definition: A free, open-source, fully-local push-to-talk dictation app. Transcription runs on your machine — no cloud, no subscription, nothing leaves your laptop. It's the on-ramp to voice coding for anyone who wants to keep their words on their own hardware. (handy.computer)

Hold a key, speak, release. The held key keeps voice deliberate, so the agent gets clean intent instead of background chatter. You decide exactly when you're addressing the model and when you're just thinking out loud.
The shift voice forces: state the outcome you want instead of describing edits character by character. You stop narrating the typing and start naming the result. The agent fills in the keystrokes.
How fast you get an idea out of your head and into the agent. Typing tops out near 40–80 wpm; speaking runs near 150. Same brain, roughly triple the throughput. That gap is the whole case for voice.

More than one agent running at once, each on its own task, with you directing. Voice makes a fleet practical — you brief the next agent while the last one works. One operator, several agents, all moving.
terms.hear: “I've got three agents going right now.”

sections.name: Agentic loops
sections.intro: A model alone can only talk. A loop is how it acts — try, check, fix, repeat — until the work meets the bar you set.

terms.definition: Try → check → fix → repeat, automatically, until the result meets the bar you set. The shift that matters: you stop iterating by hand and start authoring the loop that iterates for you.
terms.hear: “My job is to write loops now.”

terms.definition: Your process, written down as steps an agent can run: inputs, steps, outputs, and what “done” means. The single best thing you can do this month is write down one workflow you're sick of doing by hand.
An agent that an agent spins up to handle a piece of the work — research this, summarize that — in parallel, each with its own clean context. How big jobs stay fast and focused.
The coordinator of several agents working one bigger job: it hands out work, checks results, and decides what happens next. An agent is one worker; an orchestrator runs the crew.
A scored, repeatable test of AI quality — the same questions, graded the same way, run every time something changes. How you know your AI system got better instead of just different.

The slash command that runs a prompt on a schedule or lets the model self-pace until a condition is met. A goal has a finish line; a loop runs until the work is dry. Reach for it when “keep going until X” is the instruction.

A multi-step job handed to an agent as an explicit checklist. “Done” is every box checked, not the model's say-so — which stops it declaring victory early. Write the checklist and the agent has something concrete to prove it finished.

A workflow whose steps are decided as it runs — the agent fans out, branches, and synthesizes from what it finds instead of following a fixed script. The shape of the work is discovered on the way, not drawn up front. Use it when you can't list every step in advance.

A separate, isolated checkout of the same repo (git worktree). Give each parallel agent its own worktree and they never collide — no stepping on each other's files, no merge chaos mid-run. It's how a fleet works the same project at once.

Code that fires automatically on an event — every edit, before a commit, on session end. The cheapest verification gate there is: it runs without anyone remembering to run it. Wire one up once and it guards every change after.

Anything an agent's work must pass before it counts as done — a hook, a test suite, a second model's review. Operators trust the gates, not the model's confidence. The agent doesn't get to grade its own homework.

Handing a change to a second, independent model (e.g. the Codex CLI) tasked with finding what's wrong. Two models disagreeing catch what one talks itself past. The second opinion is the point — you want it looking for flaws, not nodding along.

sections.name: Scheduled tasks
sections.intro: The loop you ran by hand, now running itself — on a timer, while you're somewhere else. This is where the work stops needing you in the room.

terms.definition: Work that runs on a timer instead of when you're at the keyboard — nightly, every Monday, on a cron. The loop you ran by hand, now running itself. You come back to finished work and evidence, not a blank prompt.
The syntax for “run this on a schedule.” “0 6 * * 1” is 6am every Monday. You say when; the agent sets it. Five fields — minute, hour, day, month, weekday — and you've described any recurring time.

An agent running with no human watching — kicked off by a schedule, an event, or a one-line request, reporting back when finished. You kick off the task, it goes end to end, and you judge the evidence it brings back.

The AI-native engineer's stance: you don't type the code, you direct the agents that do — by voice, in loops, behind gates, on a schedule. The work shifts from writing every line to setting the goal, the gates, and the timer.
terms.hear: “I'm running the agents, not the keyboard.”

sections.name: Under the hood
sections.intro: For the curious: the machinery underneath everything above. None of it is required to be an operator — but it's good to know what you're standing on.

terms.definition: The kind of model Claude is — one trained primarily on language. When someone says “the LLM,” they mean the model.

Two phases. Training: the model reads an enormous share of everything humans have ever written — in a loop, over and over, adjusting billions of internal dials until its predictions stop being wrong. That takes months and data centers. Inference: the dials freeze, and you use the trained network to make new predictions — every single time you press Enter.

Modern models can think privately before answering — working through a problem step by step, then giving you the conclusion. More thinking time costs more tokens and produces noticeably better answers on hard problems.
A Claude keyword: include the word “ultrathink” in a request and Claude spends substantially more thinking effort on it. One word, deeper work — use it on problems that deserve it.
terms.hear: “I added ultrathink and it actually caught the edge case.”

terms.definition: One of the handful of current best models from a top lab (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google). The frontier moves every few months, which is why advice from last year can be stale.

Text converted into a long list of numbers that captures its meaning, so a computer can measure that two sentences are about the same thing even when they share no words. It's how you find every passage about returns even when nobody typed the word “returns.”

A pattern: search your own documents first, hand the best matches to the model, then have it answer. It's how an AI answers accurately from material it was never trained on — your docs, your notes, your codebase.
Deciding what goes in front of the model: which files, which documents, which instructions, in what order. Most of the difference between “AI is magic” and “AI is useless” is context engineering.
Standing instructions the model receives before your message — its job description for the conversation. You experience it as “why Claude behaves consistently.”

A plain-text file of standing instructions that Claude Code reads automatically at the start of every session in a project. It's where a team writes down “how we do things here” — and where the odd things only your experts know should go.
terms.hear: “Put it in the CLAUDE.md so it stops making that mistake.”

terms.definition: Notes Claude keeps across sessions — facts about you, your project, your preferences — so you stop repeating yourself. Different from the context window: memory persists, context resets.

A reusable playbook, written in plain language, that teaches Claude to do a specific task your way — “how we review a change,” “how we write release notes.” Written once, shared with everyone, improved over time.
Type / in Claude Code and a menu of your installed skills and commands pops up. It's how you invoke a skill on purpose instead of hoping Claude picks it up.
A bundle of skills, tools, and settings you install as a unit. An ideation plugin that interviews you before any work starts is one example.

A skill that interviews you, relentlessly, until it's confident it understands what you actually want — before any work starts. It's the cheapest insurance there is against an AI confidently building the wrong thing.
terms.hear: “Run ideation first — don't burn tokens guessing.”

terms.definition: How one piece of software asks another to do something — no screens, no clicking, just structured requests and answers. When someone “calls the Claude API,” their program is sending prompts and getting work back with no chat window involved.
What you ask the model. The skill of prompting is mostly the skill of being clear: state the outcome you want and what “done” looks like, not the steps you'd personally take.
